questions.question:
The worst case running times of Insertion sort, Merge sort and Quick sort, respectively are:
A. Theta(n log n), Theta(n log n) and Theta(n^2)
B. Theta(n^2), Theta(n^2) and Theta(n log n)
C. Theta(n^2), Theta(n log n) and Theta(n log n)
D. Theta(n^2), Theta(n log n) and Theta(n^2)
questions.answer: D. Theta(n^2), Theta(n log n) and Theta(n^2)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexities of Insertion sort, Merge sort, and Quick sort

understand.plain: Each sorting algorithm has a worst-case scenario where it takes the maximum time. This question asks us to identify the correct worst-case complexities for three fundamental sorting algorithms. Insertion sort and Quick sort both degrade to O(n^2) in the worst case, while Merge sort always runs in Theta(n log n).

keywords.explain: The maximum time an algorithm can take over all possible inputs of size n.
keywords.example: Insertion sort on a reverse-sorted array takes Theta(n^2) comparisons.

keywords.explain: Tight bound — the algorithm takes exactly this order of time (upper and lower bounds match).
keywords.example: Merge sort is Theta(n log n) because it always splits and merges n log n times.

understand.visual_alt: Three columns: Insertion sort -> Theta(n^2), Merge sort -> Theta(n log n), Quick sort -> Theta(n^2) in worst case.

given.aim: Identify worst-case time complexity of Insertion sort, Merge sort, and Quick sort.

terms.meaning: Occurs when input is in reverse sorted order; every new element must be compared with all previously sorted elements.
Merge sort always divides into two halves and merges — the structure never changes regardless of input.
terms.example: Even a sorted array takes the same n log n steps.
terms.connects: This gives Theta(n log n) in all cases.

terms.meaning: Occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g., already-sorted input with first/last element as pivot), causing maximally unbalanced partitions.
terms.example: Sorted array with last element as pivot: partitions of size n-1 and 0 at each step.

given.plan: Recall the established worst-case complexities for each algorithm: Insertion sort degrades to Theta(n^2) on reverse-sorted input, Merge sort is always Theta(n log n) due to its fixed divide-and-conquer structure, and Quick sort degrades to Theta(n^2) when partitions are maximally unbalanced.

steps.title: Insertion Sort Worst Case
steps.apply: For reverse-sorted input, inserting the i-th element requires comparing it with all i-1 already-sorted elements. Total comparisons = 1+2+...+(n-1) = n(n-1)/2 = Theta(n^2).
steps.note: Best case for insertion sort is Theta(n) on already-sorted input.

steps.title: Merge Sort Worst Case
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Theta(n) => T(n) = Theta(n log n)
steps.apply: Merge sort always divides the array into two equal halves and merges them. The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n solves to Theta(n log n) by the Master theorem (case 2). This holds for ALL inputs.
steps.note: Merge sort has the same complexity for best, average, and worst cases.

steps.title: Quick Sort Worst Case
steps.apply: When pivot is always the minimum or maximum element, partitioning produces one subarray of size n-1 and one of size 0. Recurrence: T(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n), which solves to Theta(n^2).
steps.note: Average case of Quick sort is Theta(n log n) with random pivot.

steps.title: Match to Options
steps.apply: Insertion sort: Theta(n^2), Merge sort: Theta(n log n), Quick sort: Theta(n^2). This matches option D.
steps.note: Option C is wrong because it puts Theta(n log n) for Quick sort (that's only the average case).

solution.result: D. Theta(n^2), Theta(n log n) and Theta(n^2)

questions.formula_note: Worst-case analysis: Insertion sort and Quick sort degrade to Theta(n^2); Merge sort is always Theta(n log n) due to its fixed divide-and-conquer structure.

questions.question:
Assume that the algorithms considered here sort the input sequences in ascending order. If the input is already in ascending order, which of the following are TRUE?
I. Quicksort runs in Theta(n^2) time
II. Bubblesort runs in Theta(n^2) time
III. Mergesort runs in Theta(n) time
IV. Insertion sort runs in Theta(n) time

A. I and II only
B. I and IV only
C. II and IV only
D. I and IV only
questions.to_find: Which statements about sorting algorithm behavior on already-sorted input are TRUE

understand.plain: When the input array is already sorted in ascending order, some sorting algorithms become faster (best case) while others remain slow. This question tests understanding of how each algorithm behaves on sorted input. We need to evaluate each statement I through IV against the actual behavior of each sorting algorithm.

keywords.explain: The minimum time an algorithm takes over all inputs of size n, often achieved by specially structured inputs like already-sorted arrays.
keywords.example: Insertion sort on a sorted array only needs n-1 comparisons (best case Theta(n)).

keywords.explain: Quicksort performance heavily depends on the choice of pivot; choosing the first or last element on a sorted array causes worst-case behavior.
keywords.example: With last-element pivot on [1,2,3,4,5], each partition splits as (n-1) and (0), giving Theta(n^2).

understand.visual_alt: Table showing each algorithm's behavior on sorted input: Quicksort (last/first pivot) -> Theta(n^2), Bubblesort -> Theta(n) with optimization, Mergesort -> Theta(n log n), Insertion sort -> Theta(n).

given.aim: Determine which sorting algorithms exhibit stated complexities when given an already-sorted ascending array.

terms.meaning: With first or last element as pivot, sorted input causes maximally unbalanced partitions: one subarray of size n-1 and one of size 0.
terms.connects: Statement I: Quicksort runs in Theta(n^2) is TRUE.

terms.meaning: With a standard implementation, the first pass makes n-1 comparisons and finds no swaps; an optimized version terminates after one pass. Standard bubblesort still does Theta(n^2) comparisons; optimized does Theta(n).
terms.example: Optimized bubble sort: after one pass with zero swaps, terminates in O(n).
terms.connects: Statement II: 'Bubblesort runs in Theta(n^2)' — for the standard (non-optimized) version this is true but GATE typically assumes the optimized version; hence Statement II is FALSE (it runs in Theta(n)).

terms.meaning: Mergesort always divides and merges regardless of input order; it cannot detect the input is already sorted.
terms.example: Merge of [1,2] and [3,4] still requires O(n) time even though both halves are sorted.
terms.connects: Statement III: Mergesort runs in Theta(n) is FALSE; it always takes Theta(n log n).

terms.meaning: For each element, it compares once with its left neighbor and immediately finds its correct position (no shifting needed).
terms.example: In [1,2,3,4,5], inserting 3 compares only with 2 and stops.
terms.connects: Statement IV: Insertion sort runs in Theta(n) is TRUE.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement by analyzing the algorithm's behavior on a sorted array: Quicksort with typical pivot strategy becomes worst case, Bubblesort with optimization becomes best case (Theta(n)), Mergesort is unchanged at Theta(n log n), Insertion sort becomes best case Theta(n).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: Quicksort on sorted input
steps.apply: When input is sorted and pivot is chosen as last element, each partition gives subarrays of sizes (n-1) and 0. Recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n) solves to Theta(n^2). Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: This assumes the standard pivot = first or last element strategy, which is the default assumption in GATE.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: Bubblesort on sorted input
steps.formula_raw: Optimized: T(n) = Theta(n); Standard: T(n) = Theta(n^2)
steps.apply: Optimized bubblesort checks after each pass if any swap occurred; on sorted input, the first pass makes n-1 comparisons with no swaps and terminates => Theta(n). GATE standard: bubblesort on sorted input runs in Theta(n), not Theta(n^2). Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: GATE typically considers the optimized bubblesort that terminates early when no swaps occur.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III: Mergesort on sorted input
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Theta(n) = Theta(n log n) always
steps.apply: Mergesort's structure is input-independent: it always divides into halves and merges. It cannot 'skip' the merging step even if subarrays are sorted. Statement III (runs in Theta(n)) is FALSE.
steps.note: Mergesort has no best-case shortcut; it's Theta(n log n) for all inputs.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement IV: Insertion sort on sorted input
steps.apply: For sorted input, each element is already in its correct position relative to the already-sorted subarray. Only 1 comparison is needed per element. Total = n-1 comparisons = Theta(n). Statement IV is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the best case for insertion sort.

steps.title: Identify correct option
steps.apply: TRUE statements: I and IV. This corresponds to option B.
steps.note: Note: Some versions of this question list option B and D both as 'I and IV only' — the answer is I and IV only.

questions.formula_note: Best-case analysis of sorting algorithms: Quicksort degrades to Theta(n^2) on sorted input (unbalanced partitions); Insertion sort achieves Theta(n) best case; Mergesort is always Theta(n log n); optimized Bubblesort achieves Theta(n) on sorted input.

questions.question:
Consider the following array:
[23, 32, 45, 69, 72, 78, 89, 97]
Which algorithm out of the following options uses the least number of comparisons (among the array elements) to sort the above array in ascending order?
A. Selection sort
B. Mergesort
C. Insertion sort
D. Quicksort using the last element as pivot
questions.to_find: Which sorting algorithm makes the fewest comparisons on the given already-sorted array

understand.plain: The input array [23, 32, 45, 69, 72, 78, 89, 97] is already sorted in ascending order. We need to find which sorting algorithm takes the fewest comparisons on this specific input. Since the array is already sorted, some algorithms can detect this early and do minimal work.

keywords.explain: An array where all elements are in the required order before sorting begins; this is the best case for some algorithms.
keywords.example: [23, 32, 45, 69, 72, 78, 89, 97] is already sorted in ascending order.

keywords.explain: The number of times two array elements are compared (less-than, greater-than) during execution of the sorting algorithm.
keywords.example: Insertion sort on sorted [1,2,3] makes only 2 comparisons (one per element after the first).

understand.visual_alt: Array [23, 32, 45, 69, 72, 78, 89, 97] — already sorted. Insertion sort makes only n-1 = 7 comparisons; other algorithms make many more.

given.aim: Determine which sorting algorithm makes the minimum number of comparisons on the array [23, 32, 45, 69, 72, 78, 89, 97] (n=8, already sorted).

terms.meaning: [23, 32, 45, 69, 72, 78, 89, 97], n = 8, already sorted in ascending order.
terms.example: Every element a[i] < a[i+1], so no element needs to move.
terms.connects: The sorted state determines which algorithm benefits the most.

terms.meaning: Each element needs only 1 comparison to confirm it's already in place.
terms.example: Inserting 32: compare with 23 (32 > 23, stop). Only 1 comparison per element.
terms.connects: Total comparisons = n-1 = 7 (minimum possible for a comparison-based sort).

terms.meaning: Selection sort: always O(n^2) comparisons regardless. Mergesort: always Theta(n log n) comparisons. Quicksort with last pivot: degrades to Theta(n^2) on sorted input.
terms.connects: All three alternatives make far more comparisons than insertion sort.

given.plan: Count comparisons for each algorithm on the sorted 8-element array: Insertion sort = 7, Selection sort = 28, Mergesort ~ 17 (using n log n estimate with merge comparisons), Quicksort with last pivot ~ 28 (worst case). Insertion sort wins with just n-1 comparisons.

steps.title: Insertion sort on sorted array (n=8)
steps.apply: For each element from index 1 to 7, compare with its immediate left neighbor. Since the array is sorted, a[i] > a[i-1] always, so only 1 comparison per element and we stop. Total = 7 comparisons.
steps.note: This is the absolute minimum — insertion sort on a sorted array is optimal.

steps.title: Selection sort on sorted array (n=8)
steps.apply: Selection sort always scans the remaining unsorted portion to find the minimum, regardless of whether the array is already sorted. It makes 7+6+5+4+3+2+1 = 28 comparisons.
steps.note: Selection sort has no mechanism to detect a sorted array early.

steps.title: Mergesort on sorted array (n=8)
steps.formula_raw: Comparisons ~ n log_2(n) / 2 to n log_2(n)
steps.apply: Mergesort always recurses and merges. For n=8, there are 3 levels. Each merge of two sorted halves of total size k requires between k/2 and k-1 comparisons. Total comparisons: approximately 17 (exact count depends on implementation but well above 7).
steps.note: Mergesort cannot skip work on already-sorted input.

steps.title: Quicksort (last element pivot) on sorted array (n=8)
steps.apply: With last element as pivot on sorted array: pivot is always the largest element, creating partitions of size (n-1) and 0. At each level, all remaining elements are compared with the pivot. Comparisons: 7+6+5+4+3+2+1 = 28.
steps.note: This is the worst case for quicksort.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Comparisons: Insertion sort = 7, Mergesort ~ 17, Selection sort = 28, Quicksort = 28. Insertion sort uses the fewest comparisons.
steps.note: The answer is C. Insertion sort.

questions.formula_note: Best-case behavior of insertion sort on sorted input: only n-1 comparisons (one per element to confirm it is already in place), which is optimal for any comparison-based sort.

questions.question:
An array [82, 10, 111, 75, 33, 131, 44, 93, 1] is heapified. Which one of the following options represents the first three elements in the heapified array?
A. 82, 10, 1
B. 131, 93, 111
C. 131, 111, 93
D. 131, 111, 75
questions.to_find: The first three elements of the max-heap formed from the array [82, 10, 111, 75, 33, 131, 44, 93, 1]

understand.plain: Heapifying an array means rearranging it so it satisfies the heap property. For a max-heap, every parent node is greater than or equal to its children. The first element (index 0) is always the maximum element. We need to run the heapify procedure and determine what the first three positions look like.

keywords.explain: The process of rearranging an unsorted array into a valid max-heap by calling max-heapify on all non-leaf nodes from the last non-leaf to the root.
keywords.example: For array [3,1,2], heapify gives [3,1,2] which is already a valid max-heap.

keywords.explain: For a node at index i, its children at 2i+1 and 2i+2 must both be <= a[i]. The root (index 0) holds the maximum element.
keywords.example: In [10, 5, 8, 3, 4], parent 10 > children 5 and 8; parent 5 > children 3 and 4.

class="bg"/><text x="165" y="231" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">75</text><text x="240" y="18" text-anchor="middle" class="tag mut">Max-Heap: first 3 elements = 131, 93, 111</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Max-heap tree built from [82,10,111,75,33,131,44,93,1]. Root=131, left child=93, right child=111. Final heap array: [131, 93, 111, 75, 33, 82, 44, 10, 1]. First three elements: 131, 93, 111.

given.aim: Apply Build-Max-Heap to [82, 10, 1, 111, 75, 33, 131, 44, 93] and find the first three elements of the resulting heap array.

terms.example: Index 0: 82, Index 1: 10, Index 2: 111, Index 3: 75, Index 4: 33, Index 5: 131, Index 6: 44, Index 7: 93, Index 8: 1
terms.connects: This is the array to be heapified into a max-heap.

terms.meaning: For n=9 elements, the last non-leaf is at index floor((9-2)/2) = floor(7/2) = 3, which holds value 75.
terms.example: Nodes at indices 4,5,6,7,8 are leaves (no children in the array).
terms.connects: We call max-heapify starting from index 3 down to index 0.

terms.meaning: Procedure that fixes the heap property at a node by comparing it with its children and swapping with the larger child if needed, then recursing downward.
terms.example: Heapify at node with value 10 (index 1): children are 75 (index 3) and 33 (index 4). 75 > 33 > 10, swap 10 and 75.
terms.connects: Applied bottom-up to build the full max-heap.

given.plan: Run Build-Max-Heap: call max-heapify on indices 3, 2, 1, 0 in order. Track all swaps and determine the final array. Then read the first three positions.

steps.title: Initial array and heap structure
steps.note: Last non-leaf index = floor((9-2)/2) = 3.

steps.title: Heapify at index 3 (value 75)
steps.apply:
Node 3 = 75, children: 7(93), 8(1). Max child = 93 at index 7. Swap index 3 and 7.
Array: [82, 10, 111, 93, 33, 131, 44, 75, 1]
Recursively heapify at index 7 (value 75): no children in range. Stop.
steps.note: 93 > 75, so 93 bubbles up to index 3.

steps.title: Heapify at index 2 (value 111)
steps.apply:
Node 2 = 111, children: 5(131), 6(44). Max child = 131 at index 5. Swap: index 2 and index 5.
Array: [82, 10, 131, 93, 33, 111, 44, 75, 1]
Recursively heapify at index 5 (value 111): children would be at 11 and 12, out of bounds. Stop.
steps.note: After swap: index 2 now holds 131, index 5 holds 111.

steps.title: Heapify at index 1 (value 10)
steps.apply:
Node 1 = 10, children: 3(93), 4(33). Max child = 93 at index 3. Swap: index 1 and index 3.
Array: [82, 93, 131, 10, 33, 111, 44, 75, 1]
Recursively heapify at index 3 (value 10): children 7(75), 8(1). Max = 75. Swap index 3 and 7.
Array: [82, 93, 131, 75, 33, 111, 44, 10, 1]
Recursively heapify at index 7 (value 10): no children (children would be at 15,16 - out of bounds). Stop.
steps.note: After this step, index 1 subtree satisfies max-heap property with 93 at index 1.

steps.title: Heapify at index 0 (value 82)
steps.apply:
Node 0 = 82, children: 1(93), 2(131). Max child = 131 at index 2. Swap: index 0 and index 2.
Array: [131, 93, 82, 75, 33, 111, 44, 10, 1]
Recursively heapify at index 2 (value 82): children 5(111), 6(44). Max = 111 at index 5. Swap index 2 and 5.
Array: [131, 93, 111, 75, 33, 82, 44, 10, 1]
Recursively heapify at index 5 (value 82): children at 11,12 - out of bounds. Stop.
Final array: [131, 93, 111, 75, 33, 82, 44, 10, 1]
steps.note: After complete Build-Max-Heap, the first three elements are 131, 93, 111.

steps.title: Verify final heap
steps.apply:
Final array: [131, 93, 111, 75, 33, 82, 44, 10, 1]
Verify max-heap property:
- Root 131 > children 93(index1) and 111(index2): ok
- Node 93 > children 75(index3) and 33(index4): ok
- Node 111 > children 82(index5) and 44(index6): ok
- Node 75 > children 10(index7) and 1(index8): ok
First three elements: 131, 93, 111.
steps.note: The heap is valid. First three elements are 131, 93, 111 matching option B.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.note: The first three elements of the heapified array are 131, 93, 111.

questions.formula_note: Build-Max-Heap algorithm: call max-heapify bottom-up from last non-leaf (index floor((n-2)/2)) to root (index 0); each heapify swaps a node with its largest child and recurses downward.

questions.question: Let A be an array containing integer values. The distance of A is defined as the minimum number of elements in A that must be replaced with another integer so that the resulting array is sorted in non-decreasing order. The distance of the array A = [2, 5, 3, 1, 4, 2, 6] is ___________.
questions.to_find: The minimum number of elements to replace in [2, 5, 3, 1, 4, 2, 6] to make it sorted in non-decreasing order

understand.plain: The 'distance' of an array is the minimum number of elements we need to replace (with any integer values) so that the array becomes non-decreasing. If we can keep k elements in their original positions (in the same relative order) without violating the sorted order, then we need to replace n-k elements. This is equivalent to finding the maximum number of elements we can keep, which means finding the Longest Non-Decreasing Subsequence (LNDS) — but since we keep them in original positions and replace the rest with suitable values, we need the Longest Non-Decreasing Subsequence. Actually it's the Longest Increasing Subsequence (since we can choose any replacement value, we need the elements we keep to themselves form a non-decreasing sequence in their original positions).

keywords.explain: The minimum number of elements to replace so the array becomes non-decreasing. Equivalently, n minus the length of the longest non-decreasing subsequence that can be kept.
keywords.example: For [1, 3, 2], keeping [1,2] (non-decreasing) requires replacing only 3. Distance = 1.

keywords.explain: The longest subsequence of the array (maintaining original order) where each element is >= the previous one. Elements not in this subsequence need to be replaced.
keywords.example: In [2,5,3,1,4,2,6]: subsequence [2,3,4,6] has length 4 and is non-decreasing.

stroke="#D85A30"/><text x="390" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">2</text><text x="390" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">replace</text><rect x="430" y="30" width="50" height="50" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="455" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">6</text><text x="455" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">keep</text><text x="240" y="18" text-anchor="middle" class="tag mut">A=[2,5,3,1,4,2,6]: keep {2,3,4,6}, replace {5,1,2} -> distance=3</text><text x="240" y="110" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">LNDS length = 4 (e.g. 2,3,4,6) => distance = 7 - 4 = 3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array [2,5,3,1,4,2,6] with 7 elements. Keeping the subsequence [2,3,4,6] (length 4) allows replacing the 3 remaining elements (5, 1, 2) to achieve a sorted array. Distance = 7 - 4 = 3.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of elements to replace in A = [2, 5, 3, 1, 4, 2, 6] to make it non-decreasing.

terms.connects: We need to find the minimum replacements, equivalently the maximum elements we can keep.

terms.meaning: We can replace any element with any integer. So elements we keep must themselves form a non-decreasing subsequence in their original positions; replaced elements can be set to any fitting value.
terms.example: Keep [2, 3, 4, 6] at positions 0,2,4,6; replace positions 1,3,5 with values like 2.5, 3.5, 5 to fill in.
terms.connects: Distance = n - (length of longest non-decreasing subsequence we can keep).

terms.meaning: The maximum number of elements from A that form a non-decreasing sequence (in their original left-to-right order).
terms.example: For A=[2,5,3,1,4,2,6]: [2,5,6] has length 3; [2,3,4,6] has length 4; [1,4,6] has length 3.
terms.connects: If LNDS length = L, then distance = n - L = 7 - L.

given.plan: Find the length L of the Longest Non-Decreasing Subsequence (LNDS) of A = [2, 5, 3, 1, 4, 2, 6]. Then distance = 7 - L. Use dynamic programming: dp[i] = length of LNDS ending at index i.

steps.title: Set up DP for LNDS
steps.formula_raw: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for all j < i where A[j] <= A[i]; dp[i] = 1 if no such j
steps.apply:
A = [2, 5, 3, 1, 4, 2, 6] at indices 0..6.
Compute dp[i] = length of longest non-decreasing subsequence ending at A[i].
steps.note: Non-decreasing means A[j] <= A[i] (not strictly less than).

steps.title: Compute dp values
steps.note: The LNDS has length max(dp) = 4, achieved at index 6.

steps.title: Identify the LNDS
steps.note: Other LNDS of length 4 may exist; all give the same distance.

steps.title: Compute distance
steps.apply:
n = 7, LNDS length = 4.
Distance = 7 - 4 = 3.
The 3 elements to be replaced are at positions 1 (value 5), 3 (value 1), and 5 (value 2).
After replacement: e.g. [2, 3, 3, 3.5, 4, 5, 6] which is non-decreasing.
steps.note: The replaced values can be any integers/reals that fit between neighbors; only the count (3) matters.

questions.formula_note: Distance of array = n minus the length of the Longest Non-Decreasing Subsequence (LNDS); LNDS computed using DP in O(n^2) or O(n log n).
